Delaware County Courthouse

**Media** is a [borough](/wiki/Borough_(Pennsylvania) "Borough (Pennsylvania)") in and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Delaware County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Delaware_County,_Pennsylvania "Delaware County, Pennsylvania"), United States.[[5]](#cite_note-GR6-5) It is located about 13 miles (21 km) west of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"). It is part of the [Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley"), also known as the [Philadelphia metropolitan area](/wiki/Philadelphia_metropolitan_area "Philadelphia metropolitan area").

Media was incorporated in 1850 at the same time that it was named the county seat.[[6]](#cite_note-6) As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population was 5,991.[[7]](#cite_note-Census_2010-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The history of the area goes back to [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn"), but the area remained predominantly rural until the 20th century.[[8]](#cite_note-brief-8)

### 17th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 17th century")]

Land in the area was sold and settled soon after [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn") was named proprietor of the colonial-era [Province of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Province_of_Pennsylvania "Province of Pennsylvania") in 1681 by [King Charles II of England](/wiki/King_Charles_II_of_England "King Charles II of England"). Peter and William Taylor bought the land where Media is now located, directly from Penn.[[9]](#cite_note-walking-9) At the time, the land was located in [Chester County](/wiki/Chester_County,_Pennsylvania "Chester County, Pennsylvania"). Providence Township was organized in 1684, and later divided into [Upper Providence](/wiki/Upper_Providence_Township,_Delaware_County,_Pennsylvania "Upper Providence Township, Delaware County, Pennsylvania") and [Nether Providence](/wiki/Nether_Providence_Township,_Pennsylvania "Nether Providence Township, Pennsylvania") townships by 1690, even though they only had 40 taxable properties at the time.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The current borough, formed in 1850, sits between the two townships.

In 1683, the Court of Chester County approved the construction of "Providence Great Road", now [Pennsylvania Route 252](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Route_252 "Pennsylvania Route 252"). The road, which runs north from [Chester](/wiki/Chester,_Pennsylvania "Chester, Pennsylvania") to within a few blocks of today's downtown, is shown on a 1687 map along with the names of local landowners.[[12]](#cite_note-12) It forms the eastern border of the borough.

Thomas Minshall, a [Quaker](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers"), was an early Media resident, settling just outside the small village then known as "Providence", along Providence Great Road. The village then included a tailor shop, blacksmith shop, wheelwright shop, barn and other buildings.[[13]](#cite_note-mhsh-13)

Minshall bought 625 acres (253 ha) from William Penn and arrived in 1682. The [Providence Friends Meetinghouse](/wiki/Providence_Friends_Meetinghouse "Providence Friends Meetinghouse") was established at his house in February 1688.

### 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 18th century")]

The original Friends Meetinghouse was built out of logs in 1699 or 1700, and the current building was completed in 1814. A house on Minshall's property, built around 1750, still stands and was given to the citizens of the borough in 1975.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 1789, [Chester County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Chester_County,_Pennsylvania "Chester County, Pennsylvania") was divided, with the eastern portion becoming [Delaware County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Delaware_County,_Pennsylvania "Delaware County, Pennsylvania").

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 19th century")]

The area in the center of the new county remained rural through 1850. On March 11, 1850, the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania by Special Act of Assembly incorporated the Borough of Media, and made the sale of malt and spirituous liquors unlawful within its borders. At the same time, the county seat of [Delaware County](/wiki/Delaware_County,_Pennsylvania "Delaware County, Pennsylvania") was moved to Media from Chester. The borough was formed from four farms purchased by the county, totaling only 480 acres (190 ha). The borders of the borough have not changed since that time.[[15]](#cite_note-egph-15)

Streets were plotted in a rectangular grid around the location of the new courthouse, lots were sold at public auctions, and the construction of houses began. Sources agree that Minshall Painter, a descendant of Thomas Minshall, suggested the name "Media", but do not agree on the reason. The name most likely comes from the borough's [median](/wiki/Median "Median") location in the direct center of Delaware County.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 20th century")]

In 1940, the [Pennsylvania guide](/wiki/American_Guide_Series "American Guide Series") described Media by noting that "[t]he majority of its houses, almost all built since the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), sit far back on shaded lawns and seem somewhat gloomy. The borough has a large and prosperous business section and a few small industrial plants; many townspeople work in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") or [Chester](/wiki/Chester,_Pennsylvania "Chester, Pennsylvania")."[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The [John J. Tyler Arboretum](/wiki/John_J._Tyler_Arboretum "John J. Tyler Arboretum") occupies part of Thomas Minshall's original 625 acres (253 ha). This farm and a nearby [Village of Lima](/wiki/Lima,_Pennsylvania "Lima, Pennsylvania") was used by the [Underground Railroad](/wiki/Underground_Railroad "Underground Railroad").[[18]](#cite_note-18) The land was donated to a public trust in 1944 by an eighth-generation descendant. The arboretum was started as a private collection by brothers Jacob and Minshall Painter. In 1825, they began systematically planting over 1,000 varieties of trees and shrubs. Over twenty of their original trees survive, including a [giant sequoia](/wiki/Giant_sequoia "Giant sequoia").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Minshall Painter was also a leader of the [Delaware County Institute of Science](/wiki/Delaware_County_Institute_of_Science "Delaware County Institute of Science"), which was formed on September 21, 1833, with just four other members: George Miller, John Miller, George Smith, M.D., and [John Cassin](/wiki/John_Cassin "John Cassin"). The institute was incorporated in 1836. About 1850, Painter gave the institute the land where its building currently stands at 11 Veterans Square, and the building was constructed in 1867.[[20]](#cite_note-DelCoIS-20)

In the second half of the 19th century, Media was a summer resort for well-to-do Philadelphians. The borough's large vacation hotels included the Idlewild Hotel (1871) on Lincoln Street at Gayley Terrace, Chestnut Grove House or "The Colonial" (1860) on Orange Street, and Brooke Hall on Orange Street and Washington Avenue (now Baltimore Avenue). The Chestnut Grove was used for a year by nearby [Swarthmore College](/wiki/Swarthmore_College "Swarthmore College") due to a fire on its campus.[[21]](#cite_note-Swat-21)

The [West Chester and Philadelphia Railroad](/wiki/West_Chester_and_Philadelphia_Railroad "West Chester and Philadelphia Railroad") was built through Media on October 19, 1854. Electrified service was opened on December 2, 1928. Up to 50 trains passed through each day. The railroad became part of the [Philadelphia, Wilmington and Baltimore Railroad](/wiki/Philadelphia,_Wilmington_and_Baltimore_Railroad "Philadelphia, Wilmington and Baltimore Railroad") and eventually the [Penn Central](/wiki/Penn_Central "Penn Central"). [SEPTA](/wiki/SEPTA "SEPTA") took over operations in 1983. [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") spoke at the Media Station in 1912 during his first [election campaign](/wiki/1912_United_States_presidential_election "1912 United States presidential election"). Trolley transportation lines spread to and through Media in the 1890s and early 1900s.[[22]](#cite_note-18501900a-22)[[23]](#cite_note-19001950b-23)

The Media Theatre opened as a [vaudeville](/wiki/Vaudeville "Vaudeville") house in 1927.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The first talkie film, *[The Jazz Singer](/wiki/The_Jazz_Singer "The Jazz Singer")*, was shown there. It remained a popular cinema through the 1970s and 1980s. In 1994, the theater underwent a $1 million (~$1.91 million in 2024) restoration by Walter Strine Sr. and re-opened as the Media Theatre for the Performing Arts.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Shows produced there have included *[The Full Monty](/wiki/The_Full_Monty_(musical) "The Full Monty (musical)")*, *[Carousel](/wiki/Carousel_(musical) "Carousel (musical)")* and *[Miss Saigon](/wiki/Miss_Saigon "Miss Saigon")*.

On March 8, 1971, the [Citizens' Commission to Investigate the FBI](/wiki/Citizens%27_Commission_to_Investigate_the_FBI "Citizens' Commission to Investigate the FBI") raided an [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI") "resident agency" in Media. They later released thousands of documents to major newspapers around the country. These documents revealed FBI tactics such as the illegal wiretapping of civil rights leaders like [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") and the recruitment of [Boy Scouts](/wiki/Scouting "Scouting") as informants, and confirmed for the first time the existence of [COINTELPRO](/wiki/COINTELPRO "COINTELPRO"), an FBI program to "expose, disrupt, misdirect, discredit, or otherwise neutralize" dissident groups in the United States.[[26]](#cite_note-burg-26)[[27]](#cite_note-:0-27)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In June 2006, Media became the first town in the United States to follow over 300 towns in Europe in attaining [fair trade certification](/wiki/Fair_trade_certification "Fair trade certification"). To meet the criteria for certification, Media passed a council resolution in support of [fair trade](/wiki/Fair_trade "Fair trade"), served fair-trade coffee and tea in local government meetings and offices, ensured that a range of fair-trade products were available in local restaurants and businesses, raised popular support and provided media coverage for the fair-trade campaign, and convened a fair-trade steering committee to ensure continued commitment.[[28]](#cite_note-How_now_brown_cow-28)

## Geography and climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Geography and climate")]

Media is located in central Delaware County at [39°55′8″N 75°23′17″W﻿ / ﻿39.91889°N 75.38806°W﻿ / 39.91889; -75.38806](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Media,_Pennsylvania&params=39_55_8_N_75_23_17_W_type:city) (39.918761, -75.388127).[[44]](#cite_note-GR1-44)

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the borough has a total area of 0.77 square miles (2.0 km2), of which 0.004 square miles (0.01 km2), or 0.42%, is water.[[7]](#cite_note-Census_2010-7) Media is situated on high ground (250 to 350 feet (76 to 107 m) above sea level) draining west to [Ridley Creek](/wiki/Ridley_Creek "Ridley Creek"), a south-flowing tributary of the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River").

Media has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa*) and the [hardiness zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") is 7a.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Media ZIP Code

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Media ZIP Code")]

The term "Media" is often used to include not only the borough of Media, but other municipalities but that share the [ZIP Code](/wiki/ZIP_Code "ZIP Code"). The borough of Media covers only 0.8 square miles (2.1 square kilometres) and less than 6,000 residents, but the Media ZIP Code 19063 covers 23.08 sq mi (59.8 km2) and a population of 35,704.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

According to the [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service"), the following addresses are included in the 19063 ZIP Code: [Elwyn](/wiki/Elwyn,_Pennsylvania "Elwyn, Pennsylvania"), [Garden City](/wiki/Garden_City,_Pennsylvania "Garden City, Pennsylvania"), [Glen Riddle](/wiki/Glen_Riddle,_Pennsylvania "Glen Riddle, Pennsylvania"), and [Rose Valley](/wiki/Rose_Valley,_Pennsylvania "Rose Valley, Pennsylvania").[[53]](#cite_note-53) Other areas at least partially included in the 19063 zip code are [Upper Providence Township](/wiki/Upper_Providence_Township,_Delaware_County,_Pennsylvania "Upper Providence Township, Delaware County, Pennsylvania"); [Nether Providence Township](/wiki/Nether_Providence_Township,_Pennsylvania "Nether Providence Township, Pennsylvania"), the neighborhoods of [South Media](/wiki/South_Media "South Media"), Bowling Green, Pine Ridge and Ridgewood; and most of [Middletown Township](/wiki/Middletown_Township,_Delaware_County,_Pennsylvania "Middletown Township, Delaware County, Pennsylvania"), including, Bortondale, [Riddlewood](/wiki/Riddlewood,_Pennsylvania "Riddlewood, Pennsylvania"), and [Lima](/wiki/Lima,_Pennsylvania "Lima, Pennsylvania").[[54]](#cite_note-54)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Government")]

The borough of Media is run by a mayor and an elected council. Mark Paikoff is the president of the Media Borough Council, and Elizabeth Romaine is the vice-president. As of April 2024, the other Council members are Kevin Boyer, Lisa Gelman, Jen Malkoun, Tray Herman, and Joi Washington.[[55]](#cite_note-council-55)

In 2025, Joi Washington was elected mayor, becoming the first woman to hold the office.[[56]](#cite_note-56) A member of the borough council since 2022, she won the mayoral race with 77 percent of the vote against Kevin Kellogg, a Republican.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Washington succeeds Mayor Bob McMahon, a Democrat who had served as mayor since being elected 1992. Upon taking office, Washington identified road safety and the reduction of traffic fatalities as her early priorities.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Education")]

### Primary and secondary schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Primary and secondary schools")]

Further information: [Rose Tree Media School District](/wiki/Rose_Tree_Media_School_District "Rose Tree Media School District")

Media lies within the [Rose Tree Media School District](/wiki/Rose_Tree_Media_School_District "Rose Tree Media School District"), created by a merger with the Rose Tree Union School District and Media Borough School District in 1966. Public school students living within borough boundaries attend Media Elementary School, located in Downtown Media, for grades K-5.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Springton Lake Middle School serves students in grades 6–8, and [Penncrest High School](/wiki/Penncrest_High_School "Penncrest High School") serves students in grades 9–12.

The Media-Upper Providence Friends School is the only private school in the borough.

Mother of Providence Regional Catholic School in [Wallingford](/wiki/Wallingford,_Pennsylvania "Wallingford, Pennsylvania") is the area Catholic school of the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Philadelphia](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Philadelphia "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Philadelphia"). It formed in 2012 from a merger of Nativity BVM School, which was Media's only pariochal school and St. John Chrysostom in Wallingford.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Nativity BVM school opened in 1912, with its last building occupied in 1949.[[61]](#cite_note-61) The Nativity BVM school administration chose not to file an appeal against the 2012 order to merge.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Some parents had lobbied for the continued operation of the school. The archdiocese had originally planned to make Nativity BVM the regional campus, but changed when St. John Chrystosom had appealed the decision.[[63]](#cite_note-63) After the closure, Media Elementary School occupied the campus while renovations of the permanent Media Elementary occurred.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

### Highways

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/2022-10-17_14_49_25_View_north_along_Pennsylvania_State_Route_252_%28Providence_Road%29_just_north_of_Baltimore_Pike_in_Media%2C_Delaware_County%2C_Pennsylvania.jpg/250px-2022-10-17_14_49_25_View_north_along_Pennsylvania_State_Route_252_%28Providence_Road%29_just_north_of_Baltimore_Pike_in_Media%2C_Delaware_County%2C_Pennsylvania.jpg)](/wiki/File:2022-10-17_14_49_25_View_north_along_Pennsylvania_State_Route_252_(Providence_Road)_just_north_of_Baltimore_Pike_in_Media,_Delaware_County,_Pennsylvania.jpg)

As of 2018, there were 18.29 miles (29.43 km) of public roads in Media, of which 3.40 miles (5.47 km) were maintained by the [Pennsylvania Department of Transportation](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Department_of_Transportation "Pennsylvania Department of Transportation") (PennDOT) and 14.89 miles (23.96 km) were maintained by the borough.[[69]](#cite_note-PennDOTmap-69)

[Route 252](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Route_252 "Pennsylvania Route 252"), Providence Road, is the only numbered highway presently traversing the borough. It follows a north–south alignment along the eastern border of Media.

[U.S. 1](/wiki/U.S._Route_1_in_Pennsylvania "U.S. Route 1 in Pennsylvania") formerly ran through the borough until the ["Media bypass"](/wiki/U.S._Route_1_in_Pennsylvania#Maryland_to_Interstate_476 "U.S. Route 1 in Pennsylvania") was completed in 1960.[[70]](#cite_note-70) The bypass has an unusual "volleyball" or [three-level diamond interchange](/wiki/Three-level_diamond_interchange "Three-level diamond interchange") with Interstate 476. The former Route 1 through the center of Media is known by its older name, Baltimore Avenue, changing to "[Baltimore Pike](/wiki/Baltimore_Pike "Baltimore Pike")" outside the borough limits.[[71]](#cite_note-gmPA-71)

### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Airports")]

[Philadelphia International Airport](/wiki/Philadelphia_International_Airport "Philadelphia International Airport") (PHL), the 21st-busiest airport in the nation in 2022, is 11 miles' driving distance (about a 15-minute drive) from downtown Media, following Baltimore Pike east, then [Interstate 476](/wiki/Interstate_476 "Interstate 476") south and [Interstate 95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Pennsylvania "Interstate 95 in Pennsylvania") northeast.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)

### SEPTA rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Media,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: SEPTA rail")]

* [Media station](/wiki/Media_station_(SEPTA) "Media station (SEPTA)") is a [SEPTA](/wiki/SEPTA "SEPTA") rail station of the [Media/Wawa Line](/wiki/Media/Wawa_Line "Media/Wawa Line").
* The [D1](/wiki/D_(SEPTA_Metro) "D (SEPTA Metro)") is a [SEPTA Metro](/wiki/SEPTA_Metro "SEPTA Metro") light rail line that runs through Media to the [69th Street Transportation Center](/wiki/69th_Street_Transportation_Center "69th Street Transportation Center") in [Upper Darby](/wiki/Upper_Darby "Upper Darby"). The trolley line ends in Media at Media-Orange Street station.
